does: This paper introduces Lita, a minimalist agent framework designed to uncover the authentic coding capabilities of large language models without reliance on complex scaffolding. Lita operationalizes a principle called liteness, which strips away hand-crafted workflows and elaborate tool sets to identify only the essential components needed for autonomous code generation. The framework maintains full autonomy while reducing manual design overhead, allowing researchers to conduct unified benchmarking across models and providers. By removing the confounding effects of custom prompts and intricate pipelines, Lita provides cleaner performance measurements that better reflect an LLM's genuine coding proficiency rather than the quality of engineering around it.
connects: This work responds to the proliferation of complex, task-specific code agent designs that have emerged as LLMs have been applied to programming tasks. It builds on prior research in autonomous agents and code generation that has explored tool use, multi-step reasoning, and elaborate prompting strategies, but identifies a critical limitation: the heavy reliance on proprietary evaluation frameworks and custom scaffolding makes it difficult to fairly compare model capabilities across vendors. The paper acknowledges how companies like OpenAI and Anthropic publish opaque benchmark results, and connects to broader concerns in the field about prompt engineering sensitivity, benchmark validity, and the challenge of isolating true model capabilities from engineering choices.
enables: Lita creates a foundation for more transparent and standardized evaluation of coding capabilities across LLM providers, enabling direct comparison of foundational models without confounding factors. This minimalist framework supports the development of leaner, more maintainable agent systems that reduce implementation complexity and associated costs. The work opens pathways for investigating which design components genuinely contribute to code generation performance, potentially informing more principled approaches to agent design. It also facilitates research into data leakage risks in proprietary evaluations and could establish community standards for reporting LLM coding proficiency independent of vendor-specific engineering choices.
